network.overall_strategy: This network centers threonine formation on the strongest literature-supported prebiotic routes: cyanosulfidic Strecker synthesis via lactaldehyde and via glyceraldehyde, plus Bucherer-Bergs conversion of lactaldehyde. Hydrothermal chemistry is used conservatively as a source of reduced-carbon feedstocks and concentration mechanisms rather than as a direct high-temperature threonine-forming regime, consistent with threonine fragility. Surface UV photochemistry in Cu-cyanide/sulfide/phosphate settings produces aldehyde hubs and threonine aminonitrile, while biochemical/prebiotic stages rapidly capture unstable threonine into peptides. Ten pathways are enumerated, but lower-confidence routes are explicitly marked as mixture-source or speculative where the literature is indirect.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction to formate on iron-sulfur minerals

conditions.other: alkaline vent conditions, pH 9-11; microporous Fe(Ni)S chimney setting

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-surface reduction of CO2 by H2 yields formate as an early fixed-carbon product under vent redox gradients.
reactions.reasoning: Conservative hydrothermal entry step based on alkaline vent iron-sulfur chemistry and Hadean chimney mineralogy; used here as a feedstock/concentration bridge rather than direct threonine synthesis, consistent with threonine instability. Supported by vent geochemistry and FeS/greigite precipitation studies (White et al., 2015; Lane & Martin, 2012).

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic photoredox generation of formaldehyde, glycolaldehyde, glyceraldehyde, and acetaldehyde from HCN

conditions.catalyst: Cu(I) cyanide photoredox system with sulfide reductant; UV-exposed phosphate-buffered pools
conditions.other: UV irradiation; cyanosulfidic network; phosphate-buffered surface waters

reactions.mechanism: UV-driven Cu(I)/Cu(II) cyanide photoredox chemistry with H2S as reductant converts HCN into reduced carbonyl compounds through iterative homologation.
reactions.reasoning: This is the central literature-backed carbonyl-generating module for threonine chemistry. Ritson and Sutherland showed UV irradiation of Cu(I) cyanide with H2S generates formaldehyde, glycolaldehyde, and acetaldehyde; Patel et al. embedded these products, including glyceraldehyde-level homologation, within a broader cyanosulfidic protometabolism that also yields amino-acid precursors. Mineral agents are included to satisfy surface-mineral constraints while preserving the specific Cu-cyanide photoredox chemistry from the literature (Ritson & Sutherland, 2013; Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Acetaldehyde cyanohydrin formation
reactions.mechanism: Nucleophilic cyanide addition to acetaldehyde forms the corresponding cyanohydrin.
reactions.reasoning: Ritson and Sutherland explicitly identified acetaldehyde cyanohydrin as the precursor to lactaldehyde in the photoredox sequence. This is a standard cyanohydrin-forming step within the same surface cyanosulfidic regime (Ritson & Sutherland, 2013).

reactions.name: Photoreduction of acetaldehyde cyanohydrin to lactaldehyde

conditions.other: UV irradiation; 19% lactaldehyde yield after 2 h reported

reactions.mechanism: UV photoredox reduction of the cyanohydrin in the presence of sulfide reductant generates lactaldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: This exact intermediate-generating step is one of the best demonstrated in the threonine literature, with lactaldehyde produced in 19% yield after 2 h irradiation under Cu(I)-cyanide/H2S conditions (Ritson & Sutherland, 2013).

reactions.name: Strecker formation of threonine aminonitrile from lactaldehyde

conditions.catalyst: cyanide-rich aqueous Strecker conditions in phosphate-buffered pools
conditions.other: anoxic; NH3 forms imine with aldehyde, followed by HCN addition

reactions.mechanism: Lactaldehyde condenses with ammonia to an imine/iminium species, then cyanide adds to give 2-amino-3-hydroxybutanenitrile.
reactions.reasoning: The complete threonine route through lactaldehyde proceeds by classical Strecker chemistry to the aminonitrile, as described in detail for the cyanosulfidic network. Patel et al. also identified the threonine aminonitrile as an inevitable by-product of HCN homologation chemistry (Ritson & Sutherland, 2013; Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Direct Strecker formation of threonine aminonitrile from glyceraldehyde

conditions.catalyst: aqueous Strecker conditions; borate-stabilized sugar-rich pool

reactions.mechanism: Glyceraldehyde reacts with ammonia and cyanide to furnish the same threonine aminonitrile hub.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. explicitly identified a parallel route in which glyceraldehyde, NH3, and HCN provide the threonine aminonitrile directly, linking sugar and amino-acid precursor chemistry. Borate is included as a plausible sugar-stabilizing surface mineral consistent with the required surface-agent specification (Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of threonine aminonitrile to threonine/allothreonine mixture

conditions.other: produces racemic threonine and allothreonine diastereomeric mixture

reactions.mechanism: Nitrile hydration and hydrolysis convert the aminonitrile to the corresponding amino acid mixture.
reactions.reasoning: The literature specifically states that the threonine aminonitrile hydrolyzes to threonine and allothreonine as a racemic mixture of diastereomers. This node preserves stereochemical realism rather than overclaiming a single enantiopure target product (Ritson & Sutherland, 2013; Patel et al., 2015; Schroeder & Bada, 1977).

reactions.name: Selection of threonine from threonine/allothreonine mixture

conditions.other: physicochemical enrichment/capture of one stereochemical component from mixture; acknowledged as unresolved

reactions.mechanism: Partitioning, crystallization, or selective incorporation enriches/captures threonine from a broader stereoisomeric pool.
reactions.reasoning: This is a necessary network-connecting abstraction rather than a fully solved prebiotic reaction. The literature emphasizes that abiotic synthesis yields threonine/allothreonine racemates and that stereochemical selection remains an open problem; small ee values can in principle be amplified by evaporation/crystallization, but for threonine specifically this remains unresolved. Included explicitly as a gap-addressing speculative capture step (Modica et al., 2014; Breslow as discussed in the review; Schroeder & Bada, 1977).

reactions.name: Bucherer-Bergs conversion of lactaldehyde to hydroxyethyl hydantoin
reactions.mechanism: Lactaldehyde reacts with cyanide, ammonia, and carbonate species to form a hydantoin thermodynamic sink.
reactions.reasoning: The deep research specifically identifies the Bucherer-Bergs route as a plausible threonine pathway, with 5-(1-hydroxyethyl)hydantoin predicted from lactaldehyde. Pulletikurti et al. experimentally demonstrated analogous alpha-keto-acid to amino-acid hydantoin chemistry, while hydantoins are also meteoritically relevant (Pulletikurti et al., 2022; Lerner & Cooper, 2005).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of hydroxyethyl hydantoin to threonine/allothreonine mixture
reactions.mechanism: Hydrolytic ring opening of the hydantoin yields the corresponding amino acid mixture.
reactions.reasoning: The Bucherer-Bergs logic depends on hydantoin intermediates acting as thermodynamic sinks that later hydrolyze to amino acids. For threonine this is a literature-guided extrapolation from demonstrated hydantoin chemistry and is therefore included but flagged as pathway-completion chemistry rather than a direct published threonine experiment (Pulletikurti et al., 2022; Lerner & Cooper, 2005).

reactions.name: Spark discharge synthesis of HCN and acetaldehyde-rich precursor pool

conditions.temperature: electric discharge; ambient bulk aqueous collection
conditions.catalyst: electrical energy, not mineral-catalyzed; products collected in aqueous phase
conditions.other: CH4/N2/H2O with traces of NH3; discharge atmosphere

reactions.mechanism: Electrical discharge through reduced gases generates reactive nitriles and aldehydes that dissolve into aqueous solution.
reactions.reasoning: Ring et al. identified threonine/allothreonine in spark-discharge products from CH4/N2/H2O with traces of NH3. To avoid overcompressing direct threonine formation, this node is restricted to the mechanistically plausible precursor pool—HCN and acetaldehyde—feeding the better-resolved Strecker network (Ring et al., 1972).

reactions.name: H2S-enhanced spark discharge synthesis of HCN and acetaldehyde-rich precursor pool

conditions.temperature: electric discharge; ambient bulk aqueous collection

reactions.mechanism: Discharge in sulfur-bearing reduced gases increases production of hydroxylated and sulfur-influenced organics, including threonine-relevant precursor pools.
reactions.reasoning: Reanalysis of Miller's H2S-containing experiment showed threonine among 23 amino acids, and sulfur-bearing discharge settings favored hydroxylated amino acids. This node is again treated as a precursor-source process rather than a single-step clean threonine synthesis (Parker et al., 2011; Johnson et al., 2008).

reactions.name: Ammonia-aldehyde aqueous chemistry generating hydroxy amino acid isomer pool including threonine-related C4 products

conditions.catalyst: none established; mineral concentration surfaces included
conditions.other: mixed aldehyde-ammonia solution; low selectivity is expected

reactions.mechanism: Complex carbonyl-ammonia reactions generate families of hydroxy amino acid structural isomers, among which threonine-related C4 species are plausible components.
reactions.reasoning: Koga and Naraoka demonstrated that labeled aldehydes reacting with ammonia produce hydroxy amino acid families including C3 and C4 structural isomers relevant to meteoritic distributions. This is deliberately framed as a low-selectivity isomer-pool source and not as a clean threonine-targeted transformation (Koga & Naraoka, 2022).

reactions.name: HCN polymerization and hydrolysis yielding mixed amino-acid pool including threonine/allothreonine

conditions.catalyst: none specific; polymerization/hydrolysis in aqueous or freezing environments
conditions.other: NH4CN/HCN oligomerization and subsequent hydrolysis; highly heterogeneous chemistry

reactions.mechanism: HCN oligomerization followed by hydrolytic breakdown produces a broad amino-acid mixture.
reactions.reasoning: HCN polymer hydrolysates are known to yield amino-acid mixtures including threonine, but this route is highly nonselective. It is included only as a background mixture-source pathway, in line with the literature review and freezing experiments, not as a strong target-directed route (Ruiz-Bermejo et al., 2013; Miyakawa et al., 2002).

reactions.name: Speculative reverse threonine-aldol relationship: glycine plus acetaldehyde to threonine/allothreonine mixture

conditions.other: requires high concentrations or catalytic assistance; thermodynamically unfavorable

reactions.mechanism: Conceptual reverse of threonine retro-aldol cleavage, condensing glycine-derived nucleophile with acetaldehyde to build the beta-hydroxylated C4 skeleton.
reactions.reasoning: This route is explicitly speculative. The literature notes the structural relationship and thermodynamic difficulty, with no purely prebiotic demonstration of the specific condensation. It is included only to satisfy the documented conceptual route while clearly downgrading its confidence (Fesko, 2016).

reactions.name: Peptide capture of threonine with glycine

conditions.other: repeated wet-dry cycles in prebiotic pools; rapid utilization of unstable threonine

reactions.mechanism: Dehydration-condensation forms a peptide bond, sequestering threonine from further degradation.
reactions.reasoning: Because threonine is unusually fragile, rapid incorporation into peptides is a plausible preservation/use strategy. Wet-dry cycling and mineral-assisted peptide formation are well documented in prebiotic chemistry, and this node operationalizes the literature's central challenge of using threonine before decomposition (Huber & Wächtershäuser, 1998; Lahav et al., 1978; Damer & Deamer, 2020; Truong et al., 2019).

reactions.name: Threonine degradation by beta-elimination to dehydroaminobutyric acid

conditions.other: captures instability bottleneck of beta-hydroxyl amino acids

reactions.mechanism: Beta-hydroxyl-assisted elimination removes water to generate an unsaturated amino acid derivative.
reactions.reasoning: The literature emphasizes threonine's exceptional instability and likely beta-elimination behavior analogous to serine, while also noting that decomposition products remain underexplored. This node is therefore a literature-guided bottleneck representation with partly inferential product assignment (Truong et al., 2019; Körner, 2021).

pathways.name: Canonical cyanosulfidic lactaldehyde Strecker route
pathways.description: HCN photoredox chemistry generates acetaldehyde, which passes through cyanohydrin reduction to lactaldehyde, then Strecker synthesis and hydrolysis to the threonine/allothreonine pool, followed by threonine capture.

pathways.name: Direct glyceraldehyde Strecker route
pathways.description: Cyanosulfidic sugar chemistry produces glyceraldehyde, which directly enters Strecker formation of threonine aminonitrile.

pathways.name: Bucherer-Bergs route from lactaldehyde
pathways.description: The lactaldehyde hub is diverted into a hydantoin thermodynamic sink that hydrolyzes to the threonine/allothreonine pool.

pathways.name: Classic spark-discharge precursor route via lactaldehyde
pathways.description: Electric discharge in CH4/N2/H2O/NH3 produces HCN and acetaldehyde precursors that feed the resolved lactaldehyde Strecker sequence.

pathways.name: H2S-enhanced spark-discharge precursor route via lactaldehyde
pathways.description: Sulfur-bearing discharge chemistry produces a hydroxylated amino-acid-favorable precursor pool that flows through lactaldehyde chemistry.

pathways.name: H2S-enhanced spark-discharge to glyceraldehyde Strecker route
pathways.description: Discharge chemistry provides HCN which joins the cyanosulfidic carbonyl pool and enters the glyceraldehyde branch to threonine aminonitrile.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal-to-surface vent-fed lactaldehyde route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal formate production and vent concentration feed surface cyanosulfidic HCN chemistry, connecting vent carbon fixation to surface threonine synthesis.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal-to-surface vent-fed glyceraldehyde route
pathways.description: Vent-derived reduced carbon and concentration support surface HCN photochemistry that yields glyceraldehyde, then threonine aminonitrile.

pathways.name: Ammonia-aldehyde hydroxy amino-acid pool route
pathways.description: Mixed aldehydes and ammonia generate a low-selectivity hydroxy amino-acid isomer pool that includes threonine-related products, from which threonine is captured.

pathways.name: HCN polymer hydrolysate background route
pathways.description: HCN oligomerization and hydrolysis produce a heterogeneous amino-acid mixture containing threonine/allothreonine, followed by selection of threonine.
